nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes and lagoons with moderate light and flow; often found on protected reef faces

decorPreferences: open space on all sides from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting from supplemental target feeding once weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or zooplankton. Direct spot-feeding with a pipette or turkey baster tends to produce the best results.

goodWith: clownfish (with caution — may attempt to host frogspawn as a surrogate anemone)
other Euphyllia species (Frogspawn/Hammer/Torch can generally touch each other without harm)
non-stinging soft corals placed well out of sweeper range
avoidWith: other LPS corals placed within 15 cm
SPS corals placed within sweeper range
zoanthids and mushroom corals placed near colony
peppermint shrimp (have been known to pick at polyps)

summary: Frogspawn Coral is one of the most popular large polyp stony (LPS) corals in the reef hobby, recognized for its swaying, grape-cluster polyp tips. Generally considered intermediate in care, it needs moderate light and indirect flow, but its potent sweeper tentacles make placement critical.
careNotes:
The scientific name used in the aquarium trade is Euphyllia divisa. A 2017 taxonomic revision reclassified the species as Fimbriaphyllia divisa, but the older name has remained in widespread use among retailers and hobbyists, and is the name most commonly encountered when shopping or researching this coral.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep in moderate PAR (approximately 75–150 PAR). Excessive light tends to cause bleaching in this species. New specimens are commonly acclimated by starting lower in the rockwork and gradually moving up over 2–4 weeks.

Flow: Direct laminar flow has been reported to damage polyp tissue and cause retraction. A gentle back-and-forth swaying motion of the tentacles is the target. Randomized flow from propeller-style powerheads tends to work better than wave-makers aimed directly at the colony.

Placement: Generally advised to place in the middle zone of the aquarium, anchored securely to stable rockwork. This coral is top-heavy and does not naturally attach to rock — it should be wedged securely or secured with putty. Open clearance of at least 15 cm on all sides from other coral colonies is strongly recommended given the sweeper tentacle reach.

Aggression / Allelopathy: Frogspawn Coral is considered one of the more aggressive LPS corals in the reef hobby. Sweeper tentacles have been reported to extend 10–15 cm (up to 6 inches) beyond the colony base primarily at night. These tentacles carry potent nematocysts capable of stinging and killing neighboring LPS corals, SPS frags, zoanthids, and mushroom corals. Particularly susceptible neighbors should be kept well clear. Beyond physical stinging, Euphyllia divisa has been documented releasing chemical allelopathic compounds into the water column that can negatively affect non-Euphyllia LPS corals placed elsewhere in the tank, particularly in small or low-flow systems. Activated carbon and regular water changes are commonly recommended when keeping this species alongside sensitive LPS.

Euphyllia-friendly placement exception: Frogspawn, Hammer (Euphyllia ancora / Fimbriaphyllia ancora), and Torch (Euphyllia glabrescens) corals have generally been observed to coexist without harming each other even when tentacles touch. This cross-Euphyllia tolerance is widely reported in the hobby, though individual colonies may vary. Non-Euphyllia LPS should NOT be placed within sweeper range regardless of this exception.

Feeding: Supplemental target feeding is widely recommended even though the coral is primarily photosynthetic. Offering mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or small zooplankton once per week has been reported to improve growth rate and coloration. Do not broadcast-feed — use a pipette or turkey baster to deliver food directly to extended polyps.

Brown Jelly Disease: This species is considered particularly susceptible to brown jelly disease (bacterial infection), which presents as a rapidly spreading brown mucus over the coral tissue. New frags should be dipped before introduction.

Fragging: Propagation by fragging is practiced in captivity, though Frogspawn grows more slowly than many LPS. Bone cutters are used to separate heads at the skeletal base. Frags begin small (2–3 cm) and expand over months to full head size.

Colony forms and related species: Two skeletal forms are traded — branching Frogspawn (multiple separate branches each bearing a polyp head, and the more commonly available form) and wall Frogspawn (a continuous skeleton with many heads fused). Husbandry is identical for both. The closely related species Fimbriaphyllia paradivisa, commonly sold as 'Pink Frogspawn', shares the same care requirements as F. divisa and is kept in identical fashion in the aquarium.

Water chemistry stability: Like all LPS stony corals, Frogspawn consumes calcium and alkalinity as it calcifies its skeleton. Maintaining stable kH (avoiding swings greater than 1 dKH per day) and adequate calcium (400–450 ppm) is generally advised to support growth and prevent bleaching.
breedingNotes: Fragging — mechanical division of a colony — is the primary propagation method in captivity. Branching Frogspawn can be propagated by cutting individual branches at the skeletal junction with bone cutters, while wall Frogspawn can be divided by sawing through the skeleton between polyp heads. Sexual reproduction does occur on natural reefs through broadcast spawning, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temp 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, salinity 1.022-1.025, dKH 8-12), care level (moderate), lighting (moderate), flow (medium), placement (middle to top), and sweeper tentacle note (extends up to 6 inches). Also confirmed scientific name Euphyllia divisa and LPS classification.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Frogspawn Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR (100-150, recommended 100-125), temperature (77-84°F, wider range), alkalinity (8-9 dKH), calcium (400-450 ppm). Also confirmed care level Advanced vs. LiveAquaria's Moderate; averaged to Intermediate. Confirmed weekly meaty feeding and brown jelly disease susceptibility. Note: ReefChasers does not recommend keeping Frogspawn near Torches, which differs from the common Euphyllia-tolerance rule — this nuance was preserved in careNotes.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – Euphyllia Garden / Learning Center
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR (50-150), flow (low to moderate indirect), placement (lower thirds), care notes on stability and nutrient requirements. BRS confirms Euphyllia corals tolerate moderate nutrients better than ultra-low nutrient systems.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Euphyllia Care Requirements
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76-80°F), salinity (1.024-1.026), pH (8.1-8.4), alkalinity (8-10 dKH), calcium (400-450 ppm), magnesium (1250-1350 ppm). PAR 100-200. Confirmed moderate indirect flow and middle-lower placement. Minor disagreement on salinity upper bound (1.026 vs. 1.025 from LA); averaged to 1.025 as conservative upper.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Euphyllia divisa (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (family Euphylliidae, order Scleractinia) and documentation of 2017 reclassification to Fimbriaphyllia divisa. Also confirmed IUCN Near Threatened status and native range (Indo-Pacific, Australia, Southeast Asia, Ryukyu Islands, Solomon Islands, Fiji, Palau). Not used for husbandry parameters.
